h1Title: Acute Pharyngitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute pharyngitis is inflammation of the pharyngeal mucosa causing sore throat, typically lasting fewer than two weeks. It is among the most common reasons adults and children seek medical care, accounting for approximately 15 million US physician visits per year. Viral infection causes 70-80% of cases in adults and 50-70% in school-age children; group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) is the dominant bacterial pathogen, responsible for 15-30% of pharyngitis in children and 5-15% in adults. Accurate diagnosis of streptococcal pharyngitis matters because antibiotic treatment prevents rheumatic fever, reduces symptom duration, and limits transmission. Evidence-based Centor and McIsaac clinical decision rules guide antibiotic prescribing and reduce unnecessary use.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 15 million physician visits per year for pharyngitis in the United States (CDC); one of the top five ambulatory diagnoses
keyStats.demographics: Group A Streptococcus pharyngitis most prevalent in school-age children aged 5-15 years; viral pharyngitis affects all ages; adults account for about one-third of strep cases annually
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence of streptococcal pharyngitis in children aged 5-10 years; viral pharyngitis peaks in winter and spring across all age groups
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 600 million cases of group A Strep pharyngitis worldwide annually (WHO 2005); rheumatic fever from inadequately treated strep throat causes 470,000 new cases of rheumatic heart disease per year

definition: Acute pharyngitis (ICD-10: J02.9) is an acute inflammatory condition of the pharynx — the posterior wall of the throat from the soft palate to the hypopharynx — causing sore throat, odynophagia, and pharyngeal erythema. When tonsillar tissue is also involved the condition is termed acute tonsillopharyngitis, distinguished from isolated tonsillitis where tonsillar pathology predominates. Pharyngitis is classified by aetiology: viral (adenovirus, rhinovirus, influenza, Epstein-Barr virus, herpes simplex), bacterial (group A Streptococcus, group C/G Streptococcus, Fusobacterium necrophorum, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Corynebacterium diphtheriae), or non-infectious (gastro-oesophageal reflux, irritant exposure, vocal overuse). Pathophysiologically, viral and bacterial pathogens colonize the pharyngeal mucosa and trigger an innate immune response characterized by vasodilation, mucosal edema, exudate formation, and cytokine-mediated symptoms. Streptococcal pharyngitis (GABHS) carries the risk of non-suppurative sequelae including acute rheumatic fever and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis if untreated. Acute rheumatic fever remains a leading cause of acquired heart disease in children worldwide — responsible for approximately 470,000 new cases of rheumatic heart disease each year — making identification and treatment of GABHS pharyngitis a public health priority. The IDSA 2012 and AHA guidelines emphasize identifying and treating GABHS specifically, while avoiding antibiotics for viral pharyngitis to reduce antimicrobial resistance and adverse effects. Otolaryngology, pediatrics, and primary care physicians all manage this condition.

typesClassification.description: Adenovirus (responsible for 25-40% of pharyngitis overall), rhinovirus, coronavirus, influenza, and parainfluenza are the most common viral causes. Epstein-Barr virus (infectious mononucleosis) causes exudative tonsillitis with posterior lymphadenopathy in adolescents and young adults. Coxsackievirus causes herpangina with posterior pharyngeal vesicles predominantly in children. Herpes simplex virus causes ulcerative pharyngitis.

Streptococcus pyogenes causes 15-30% of pharyngitis in children and 5-15% in adults. Characterized by sudden onset sore throat, fever, pharyngeal exudate, anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, absence of cough. Requires antibiotic therapy to prevent acute rheumatic fever (which follows GABHS pharyngitis in 0.3-3% of untreated cases in endemic settings).

Epstein-Barr virus causes bilateral tonsillar enlargement with grey-white exudate, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and profound fatigue. Affects mainly adolescents and young adults. Amoxicillin and ampicillin should be avoided as they cause a characteristic maculopapular rash in 70-90% of EBV-infected patients.

Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes pharyngitis after oropharyngeal sexual contact; often asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic. Requires specific culture on modified Thayer-Martin medium or NAAT. Treated with ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose per CDC 2021 STI guidelines.

Non-group A streptococci cause pharyngitis clinically indistinguishable from GABHS but do not cause rheumatic fever. Rapid antigen tests are negative. Antibiotic treatment may shorten illness but is not mandatory to prevent sequelae.

primarySymptoms: Sore throat — sudden in onset for streptococcal infection, gradual for viral — characterized by pain on swallowing (odynophagia) ranging from mild discomfort to severe inability to swallow liquids.
Pharyngeal erythema with or without follicular or confluent exudate on the tonsils and posterior pharyngeal wall, most pronounced in GABHS and infectious mononucleosis.
Anterior cervical lymphadenopathy — tender enlarged lymph nodes in the anterior triangle of the neck, present in 70-90% of GABHS pharyngitis.
Fever above 38°C, more consistent in bacterial pharyngitis (present in 70-80% of GABHS) than viral (present in 40-60% of adenovirus infection).
Absence of cough — one of the Centor criteria; cough strongly suggests a viral respiratory cause rather than GABHS infection.
Headache and malaise, commonly accompanying both viral and bacterial pharyngitis, often out of proportion to the degree of throat inflammation.
Palatal petechiae — tiny red spots on the soft palate, present in up to 50% of GABHS cases; also seen in infectious mononucleosis.
Scarlatiniform rash (sandpaper-like rash spreading from the trunk) in group A Strep pharyngitis with toxigenic strains — signals scarlet fever requiring the same antibiotic regimen.
Posterior cervical lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly in infectious mononucleosis (EBV), distinguishing it from typical GABHS infection.
Voice change or muffled 'hot potato' voice with drooling suggests peritonsillar abscess or epiglottitis and warrants emergency evaluation.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden severe sore throat with fever above 38.5°C and visible white patches on tonsils in a school-age child — classic presentation of GABHS requiring rapid antigen testing.
Sore throat lasting more than 5-7 days without any cough or other respiratory symptoms — increases probability of bacterial cause.
Sore throat in an adolescent or young adult with marked fatigue and posterior lymph node swelling — suggests infectious mononucleosis (EBV).
Development of skin rash (fine, rough, sandpaper texture) starting on the trunk one to two days after sore throat onset — suggests scarlet fever from toxigenic GABHS.
Unilateral tonsillar asymmetry or the tonsil being pushed toward midline — early peritonsillar cellulitis that can progress to abscess.
emergencySigns: Drooling, difficulty opening the mouth (trismus), muffled 'hot potato' voice, and uvular deviation — peritonsillar abscess requiring urgent ENT evaluation and needle aspiration or drainage.
High fever with progressive dysphagia, respiratory distress, and stridor in a child — suspect epiglottitis; avoid examining the throat without immediate airway backup.
Unilateral neck swelling with fever and torticollis — parapharyngeal or retropharyngeal abscess requiring urgent CT and surgical drainage.

Throat pain radiating to the chest, odynophagia with pain disproportionate to clinical findings, Lemierre's syndrome signs (neck swelling, new respiratory symptoms) — indicates deep neck space infection requiring emergency imaging.
Inability to swallow fluids, signs of dehydration (dry lips, sunken eyes, no urine), or respiratory distress in any patient with severe pharyngitis — requires hospitalization.

causes.cause: Viral infection (adenovirus, rhinovirus, influenza, coronavirus, EBV)
causes.description: Adenovirus is the single most common cause of pharyngitis overall, accounting for 25-40% of cases across all ages. Rhinovirus, influenza, parainfluenza, and COVID-19 cause pharyngitis as part of broader upper respiratory syndromes. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) causes infectious mononucleosis with pharyngitis and exudate in adolescents and young adults.

The dominant bacterial cause. Transmitted via respiratory droplets or direct contact with infected saliva; incubation period 2-5 days. The most clinically significant cause of pharyngitis because untreated GABHS infection carries a 0.3-3% risk of acute rheumatic fever in endemic settings and a rare risk of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

causes.cause: Non-group A Streptococcal infection (Group C, Group G)
causes.description: Groups C and G streptococci cause pharyngitis clinically indistinguishable from GABHS but do not carry the same risk of rheumatic fever. They are more common in adults. Fusobacterium necrophorum, an anaerobe, is an underrecognized cause of pharyngitis in young adults and is associated with Lemierre's syndrome — septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein.

Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes pharyngitis after oropharyngeal sexual contact; it is often asymptomatic or mild and requires specific testing and treatment. Herpes simplex virus type 1 causes painful ulcerative pharyngitis and gingivostomatitis in primary infection.

Rare in vaccinated populations but causes severe pharyngitis with a grey adherent membrane extending onto the soft palate and uvula, cervical lymphadenopathy (bull-neck), and a potent exotoxin causing myocarditis and neuropathy. A public health emergency if identified. Prevented by diphtheria vaccination (DTaP/Td).

Postnasal drip from allergic rhinitis or sinusitis, dry air, gastro-oesophageal reflux (LPR), cigarette smoke, and vocal overuse all cause chronic or recurrent pharyngeal irritation without infection. These account for a substantial proportion of pharyngitis presentations in adults outside the winter peak.

riskFactors.description: School-age children are the primary reservoir for GABHS; the attack rate in this age group is highest, and transmission in school settings is efficient. Children in this age group are 3-4 times more likely to have GABHS pharyngitis than adults with the same symptoms.

riskFactors.factor: Close contact with an infected person
riskFactors.description: Household contacts of GABHS-positive individuals have a 25-30% risk of developing strep pharyngitis within 2-5 days. Day care and school classroom settings sustain transmission chains throughout late autumn and winter.
GABHS pharyngitis peaks from October through April in the Northern Hemisphere, coinciding with school terms. Adenoviral pharyngitis has a bimodal peak in late winter and early autumn.
HIV, organ transplantation, and systemic corticosteroid use increase susceptibility to both viral and bacterial pharyngitis and raise the risk of atypical pathogens including CMV and herpes simplex.

riskFactors.factor: History of recurrent pharyngitis or tonsillitis
riskFactors.description: Patients with 7+ episodes of pharyngitis per year meet criteria for tonsillectomy consideration per AAO-HNS guidelines. Recurrent GABHS carriage status increases family and classroom exposure.

Smoking and alcohol disrupt mucosal barrier function, impair local immunity, and are associated with a higher frequency and longer duration of pharyngitis episodes. Smokers report pharyngitis 1.5-2 times more frequently than non-smokers.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 5-15 years have the highest rates of GABHS pharyngitis; strep accounts for 30-40% of pharyngitis in this group, compared with 5-15% in adults (IDSA 2012).
Adolescents and young adults aged 15-25 are the primary demographic for infectious mononucleosis (EBV), which presents as exudative pharyngitis with extreme fatigue and posterior lymphadenopathy.
Adults over age 15 have predominantly viral pharyngitis; GABHS accounts for fewer than 15% of adult sore throat presentations.
Sexually active adults are at risk for gonococcal pharyngitis after oropharyngeal exposure; this is often asymptomatic and diagnosed only with specific STI testing.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of acute pharyngitis targets the key clinical decision: is this GABHS infection requiring antibiotics, or a viral illness requiring only symptomatic treatment? The Centor score — modified by McIsaac — assigns one point each for exudate on tonsils, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, fever history, and absence of cough, with one point subtracted for age above 45. A score of 0-1 makes GABHS unlikely (less than 5-10% probability) and no testing is needed; a score of 2-3 warrants rapid antigen detection testing (RADT); a score of 4+ may justify empirical antibiotic therapy after testing. The rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for group A Strep has sensitivity of 70-90% and specificity above 95% in children; the IDSA recommends confirmatory throat culture for all negative RADTs in children given the risk of rheumatic fever. In adults, negative RADT does not need culture confirmation because rheumatic fever risk is very low. Throat culture on blood agar remains the gold standard with 90-95% sensitivity but requires 24-48 hours. Monospot test (heterophile antibody) is used when EBV mononucleosis is suspected. EBV-specific antibodies (VCA IgM, EA) confirm the diagnosis when monospot is negative in early illness or in young children. Gonococcal pharyngitis requires specific NAAT or culture on selective medium. Diphtheria is diagnosed by culture on Loeffler and tellurite media with toxigenicity testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Stratifies probability of GABHS pharyngitis to guide testing and antibiotic prescribing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the throat and lymph nodes and asks about fever and cough. No investigations needed — the score is calculated from examination findings in under 2 minutes. Score ≤1: no testing; Score 2-3: RADT; Score ≥4: consider empirical antibiotics.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for group A Strep
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid bedside diagnosis of GABHS pharyngitis to guide antibiotic prescribing
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab is firmly rubbed on both tonsils and the posterior pharyngeal wall for 5-10 seconds — briefly uncomfortable. The swab is analyzed by immunoassay. Result available in 5-10 minutes. Sensitivity 70-90%, specificity above 95%.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for GABHS diagnosis; recommended to confirm negative RADT in children
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same tonsillar and posterior pharyngeal swab as for RADT. The swab is plated on blood agar and incubated for 18-24 hours. Results returned next day. Sensitivity 90-95% for GABHS.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for infectious mononucleosis (EBV) when clinical features suggest the diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick blood sample or standard venous draw. The test detects heterophile antibodies that appear 1-3 weeks after EBV infection. Sensitivity 85-90% in adolescents; lower in children under 12. Results within 5 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: EBV-specific antibody panel (VCA IgM, EA IgG, EBNA IgG)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms EBV mononucleosis when monospot is negative or equivocal, especially early in illness
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. VCA IgM appears within the first week of illness and is the most sensitive early marker. Results take 2-5 days from a reference laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: NAAT or culture for Neisseria gonorrhoeae
diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses gonococcal pharyngitis in patients with appropriate sexual exposure history
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Tonsillar and posterior pharyngeal swab for NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test) or culture on Thayer-Martin medium. NAAT result within 24-48 hours; culture takes 48-72 hours. Required for all patients tested for genital gonorrhoeae if there is a history of oropharyngeal exposure.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of acute pharyngitis depends critically on aetiology. For viral pharyngitis — which accounts for 70-80% of cases in adults — antibiotics are not indicated and symptomatic relief is the mainstay: analgesics, anti-inflammatory agents, throat lozenges, and adequate hydration. For confirmed or highly probable GABHS pharyngitis, antibiotic therapy serves three purposes: preventing acute rheumatic fever (the most important in high-risk populations), shortening illness duration by 1-2 days, reducing contagion period from approximately 10 days to 24 hours, and preventing suppurative complications such as peritonsillar abscess. Per IDSA 2012 guidelines, penicillin V (500 mg twice daily for 10 days in adults; 250 mg twice daily for 10 days in children) or amoxicillin (50 mg/kg once daily or 500 mg twice daily for 10 days) is first-line therapy for GABHS. Amoxicillin once daily is often preferred in children for adherence. Benzathine penicillin G intramuscular injection (1.2 million units single dose in adults; 600,000 units in children under 27 kg) is an alternative for patients with poor adherence or in settings with high rheumatic fever prevalence. For penicillin-allergic patients with non-anaphylactic allergy, first-generation cephalosporins (cefalexin) are used; for anaphylactic penicillin allergy, clindamycin or a macrolide is second-line, though macrolide resistance in GABHS exceeds 5-10% in many regions. Shorter courses (5 days) of azithromycin or clindamycin have equivalent efficacy. Fluoroquinolones are not recommended for routine pharyngitis. Systemic corticosteroids (dexamethasone 10 mg IV/IM single dose or 0.6 mg/kg in children) reduce throat pain duration by one day in severe pharyngitis and are used adjunctively when pain is severe.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin V (500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days in adults; 250 mg twice daily in children)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for GABHS pharyngitis per IDSA 2012. Group A Strep remains universally sensitive to penicillin with no documented resistance. Narrow spectrum limits disruption of gut flora compared with broad-spectrum alternatives.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents acute rheumatic fever in RCTs; reduces symptom duration by 1-2 days compared with placebo; reduces transmission to household contacts

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin (50 mg/kg once daily up to 1 g, or 500 mg twice daily for 10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative to penicillin V; slightly better tasting for children and available as suspension. Equally effective against GABHS. Must avoid in suspected EBV mononucleosis due to rash risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to penicillin V for eradication of GABHS from pharynx; once-daily dosing improves adherence in children

medicalTreatments.name: Benzathine penicillin G IM (1.2 million units single dose for adults; 600,000 units for children <27 kg)
medicalTreatments.description: Intramuscular injection providing sustained penicillin levels for 3-4 weeks. Eliminates adherence concerns and is first-line in settings with high rheumatic fever risk. Used in patients who cannot complete a 10-day oral course.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rheumatic fever prevention equivalent to oral penicillin; single-dose convenience increases treatment completion

medicalTreatments.name: Cefalexin (500 mg twice daily for 10 days) or other first-generation cephalosporins
medicalTreatments.description: First-choice alternative for patients with non-anaphylactic penicillin allergy. Meta-analyses suggest slightly higher bacteriological cure rates than penicillin. 5-10-day cephalosporin regimens show 90-95% pharyngeal eradication rates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological cure 92-95% in RCTs; slightly superior eradication rate compared with penicillin V in meta-analyses

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily or paracetamol 1 g four times daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Provide pain relief for both viral and bacterial pharyngitis. Ibuprofen has superior analgesic efficacy for sore throat compared with paracetamol in head-to-head RCTs. Appropriate for all pharyngitis patients regardless of aetiology.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Ibuprofen reduces sore throat pain scores by 30-40% at 2 hours compared with placebo; pain resolution 1 day faster vs paracetamol in RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Single-dose dexamethasone (10 mg IM or orally for adults; 0.6 mg/kg for children)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive corticosteroid for severe pharyngitis with significant odynophagia. Reduces time to complete pain resolution by approximately one day. Used alongside antibiotics in GABHS and alongside analgesics in severe viral pharyngitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces complete pain resolution time by 7.2 hours vs placebo (NNT approximately 3-5 for meaningful pain reduction at 24 hours)

surgicalOptions.name: Peritonsillar abscess drainage (needle aspiration or incision and drainage)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed by ENT under local anesthesia when peritonsillar abscess complicates pharyngitis. Needle aspiration has comparable success to incision and drainage for uncomplicated abscesses and is better tolerated.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution of abscess in 90-95% with aspiration plus antibiotics; approximately 15-20% require repeat aspiration or incision and drainage

surgicalOptions.name: Tonsillectomy (for recurrent pharyngitis)
surgicalOptions.description: Elective surgical removal of palatine tonsils. AAO-HNS guidelines recommend considering tonsillectomy for 7+ episodes per year in one year, 5+ per year for two consecutive years, or 3+ per year for three consecutive years (Paradise criteria). Also indicated for peritonsillar abscess recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces pharyngitis episodes by 50-70% in the year post-surgery compared with watchful waiting in RCTs; benefit declines over 2-3 years as children age out of peak strep susceptibility

alternativeTreatments.name: Honey (15 mL in warm water or tea)
alternativeTreatments.description: A Cochrane-reviewed meta-analysis found honey modestly reduces cough and sore throat severity in children and adults; not disease-modifying but safe as adjunct. Contraindicated in children under 12 months due to botulism risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Benzocaine or lidocaine throat lozenges and sprays
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical anesthetic lozenges provide short-term (15-20 minute) pharyngeal pain relief with good safety profile for adults and children over 12 years. Used as symptom adjuncts alongside systemic analgesics.

whySeeSpecialist: ENT referral is appropriate for recurrent pharyngitis meeting Paradise criteria for tonsillectomy, peritonsillar abscess, suspected deep neck space infection, or pharyngitis with airway compromise. An infectious disease specialist is needed for unusual pathogens, gonococcal pharyngitis in the context of STI management, or refractory infectious mononucleosis with complications.
doctorSelectionGuide: A primary care physician or pediatrician can manage the vast majority of acute pharyngitis cases. Choose an ENT for recurrent tonsillitis, abscess drainage, or surgical evaluation. For adolescents and young adults with persistent pharyngitis and fatigue, request monospot testing before considering tonsillectomy to exclude EBV mononucleosis.
hospitalCriteria: Peritonsillar abscess requiring intravenous antibiotics, incision and drainage, or airway monitoring
Retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal abscess on CT imaging
Inability to swallow fluids leading to dehydration requiring IV hydration
Suspected epiglottitis — direct laryngoscopy and airway management required in a controlled setting
Severe infectious mononucleosis with splenic enlargement and respiratory compromise
keyFacilities: Primary care and pediatric clinics with rapid strep RADT capability
ENT departments for abscess drainage, endoscopy, and tonsillectomy
Emergency departments for airway compromise, peritonsillar abscess, and systemic sepsis
GU medicine or sexual health clinics for gonococcal pharyngitis
preventionStrategies: Wash hands frequently during respiratory illness seasons and avoid touching the face, particularly the mouth and nose.
Keep children with confirmed GABHS pharyngitis home from school until 24 hours after starting antibiotics and until they are fever-free.
Replace toothbrush after completing strep treatment to avoid re-exposure from colonized bristles.
Avoid sharing eating utensils, drinking cups, and lip products with infected individuals.
Ensure adolescents and children receive age-appropriate vaccinations including annual influenza vaccine, which reduces influenza-related pharyngitis.
Test and treat asymptomatic household contacts only when there is a documented cluster of GABHS cases in a family with recurrent rheumatic fever risk, per IDSA guidance.
lifestyleModifications: Rest the voice and avoid shouting or prolonged speaking during the acute phase to reduce mucosal irritation.
Drink warm fluids (herbal tea, broth, warm water with honey) — warmth soothes pharyngeal pain and maintains hydration.
Use a humidifier in the bedroom to prevent mucosal drying from dry indoor air, particularly during winter months.
Avoid cigarette smoke and alcohol, which worsen mucosal inflammation and impair local immunity.
Gargle with warm salt water (0.9% saline) four times daily — reduces mucosal edema and provides temporary pain relief.
Take prescribed antibiotics for the full 10-day course even when feeling better at day 3-4 to ensure bacterial eradication and prevent recurrence.

recommended: Cold foods (ice cream, yogurt, ice chips) provide topical analgesia and help maintain caloric intake when swallowing is painful
Warm broths and soups provide hydration and nutrition while soothing the throat
Honey in warm water or herbal tea — has modest evidence for reducing cough and sore throat discomfort
Soft, non-irritating foods (mashed potato, oatmeal, scrambled eggs) when swallowing is difficult
avoid: Crunchy, sharp, or spicy foods that mechanically or chemically irritate the inflamed pharyngeal mucosa
Very hot beverages that can worsen mucosal inflammation during the acute phase
Alcohol, which dehydrates, impairs immunity, and worsens mucosal irritation
Acidic foods and juices (tomato, citrus) that may increase pain on swallowing in the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is recommended during the febrile acute phase. Light activity is appropriate once fever has resolved for 24 hours. Students should avoid physical education and contact sports while on antibiotics. For infectious mononucleosis, avoid all contact sports and strenuous exercise for a minimum of 3 weeks and until splenomegaly has resolved on clinical examination, due to risk of splenic rupture.
dailyManagement: Take analgesics on a regular schedule (ibuprofen 400 mg every 6-8 hours or paracetamol 1 g every 4-6 hours) rather than waiting for pain to escalate.
Gargle with warm saline (one teaspoon of salt in a glass of warm water) four times daily — reduces mucosal edema.
Maintain fluid intake above 2 L per day; use straw if swallowing is too painful to drink normally.
If prescribed antibiotics, take each dose at the same time each day to maintain therapeutic levels; do not stop early even if symptoms resolve.
Monitor for worsening symptoms — drooling, inability to open mouth, voice change, or airway distress — and seek emergency care immediately.
Stay home from work or school until fever-free for 24 hours and, for confirmed GABHS, until 24 hours after the first antibiotic dose.
prognosis: Viral pharyngitis is self-limiting; symptoms resolve in 3-7 days without treatment in the majority of patients. GABHS pharyngitis treated with penicillin resolves symptomatically within 3-5 days and bacteriologically within 10 days of treatment; untreated GABHS clears in 7-14 days in most cases but carries a risk of rheumatic fever and suppurative complications. Acute rheumatic fever follows untreated GABHS pharyngitis in 0.3-3% of cases in endemic settings and remains a leading cause of acquired heart disease in children worldwide. Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis occurs after skin or throat GABHS infection and is not prevented by antibiotics. Peritonsillar abscess complicates approximately 1 in 3,000 episodes of GABHS pharyngitis and resolves with drainage and antibiotics in over 90% of cases. Infectious mononucleosis resolves over 2-6 weeks in most patients; fatigue can persist for months in 10-15% of cases. Recurrent GABHS carriers who remain culture-positive despite treatment require re-treatment with clindamycin or amoxicillin-clavulanate targeting oropharyngeal biofilm.
recoveryTimeline: Viral pharyngitis: symptom peak day 2-3, resolution by day 5-7. GABHS with antibiotics: fever resolution within 24 hours of antibiotics, full symptom resolution by day 5-7, return to school after 24 hours on antibiotics. Infectious mononucleosis: acute symptoms 1-3 weeks, fatigue may persist 4-8 weeks; return to contact sports after 3 weeks minimum.
complications: Peritonsillar abscess — collection of pus between the tonsil capsule and pharyngeal constrictor muscle; presents with trismus, unilateral bulging, uvular deviation, and severe dysphagia; requires needle aspiration or incision and drainage.

Acute rheumatic fever — non-suppurative immune complication of GABHS pharyngitis; occurs 2-4 weeks after untreated infection; causes carditis, arthritis, Sydenham's chorea, and erythema marginatum; penicillin prophylaxis prevents recurrence.

Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis — immune-mediated kidney inflammation 1-3 weeks after GABHS pharyngitis or skin infection; presents with haematuria, proteinuria, and edema; resolves spontaneously in over 90% of children.
Scarlet fever — toxin-mediated complication of GABHS pharyngitis with characteristic rash, strawberry tongue, and fever; treated with the same antibiotic course as simple pharyngitis.

Lemierre's syndrome — septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein from oropharyngeal fusobacterial infection; presents with neck swelling, fever, and septic pulmonary emboli; high mortality if not recognized; requires prolonged IV antibiotics and anticoagulation.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery: Sore Throats
supportResources.description: Patient information on pharyngitis causes, when to see an ENT, and tonsillectomy criteria.

supportResources.name: CDC — Strep Throat
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on strep throat diagnosis, treatment, and preventing spread, including school exclusion guidance.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS — Sore Throat (acute)
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary including Centor scoring, antibiotic guidance, and self-care advice.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute tonsillitis is characterized by dominant tonsillar inflammation with enlargement, exudate, and local pain, whereas pharyngitis refers to broader posterior pharyngeal wall inflammation. In practice, GABHS infection causes tonsillopharyngitis simultaneously. Tonsillitis with peritonsillar abscess or recurrent episodes meeting Paradise criteria has specific surgical implications not shared by pharyngitis alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Common Cold (Nasopharyngitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The common cold causes predominantly nasal symptoms — watery discharge, sneezing, congestion — with mild sore throat as a secondary feature. True pharyngitis presents with sore throat as the dominant symptom, with or without exudate and lymphadenopathy. Positive rapid strep test or prominent tonsillar findings confirm pharyngitis over common cold.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epiglottitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Epiglottitis presents with severe throat pain, odynophagia, drooling, stridor, and a characteristic tripod posture in children — out of proportion to visible pharyngeal findings. It is a life-threatening emergency. Pharyngitis has proportionate findings. Do not attempt throat examination if epiglottitis is suspected; immediate airway management takes priority.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A peritonsillar abscess produces unilateral tonsillar and peritonsillar bulge, uvular deviation away from the affected side, trismus, and muffled voice — features absent in uncomplicated pharyngitis. It requires drainage rather than antibiotics alone.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tonsillar inflammation commonly accompanies pharyngitis; recurrent tonsillopharyngitis is the primary indication for tonsillectomy.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Sinusitis and pharyngitis share viral upper respiratory infection triggers and commonly occur together; postnasal drip from sinusitis causes pharyngeal irritation.

relatedConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common suppurative complication of bacterial pharyngitis/tonsillitis, requiring ENT drainage.

relatedConditions.name: Common Cold
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral pharyngitis is a feature of many common cold presentations; distinguishing between the two guides antibiotic prescribing decisions.

faqs.question: How do I know if my sore throat is strep or viral?
faqs.answer: Strep throat tends to start suddenly with fever above 38°C, visible white patches on the tonsils, and tender neck glands, without runny nose or cough. Viral sore throat usually comes with a cold — runny nose, cough, and mild throat pain. A rapid strep test (available at most clinics) gives a result in 10 minutes and is the most reliable way to distinguish them.

faqs.question: Does strep throat go away without antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Most cases of strep throat resolve on their own within 7-10 days. However, antibiotics are recommended to prevent rare but serious complications such as acute rheumatic fever, reduce symptom duration by 1-2 days, and stop you spreading strep to others. Without antibiotics, you are contagious for up to 10 days.

faqs.question: What antibiotic is best for strep throat?
faqs.answer: Penicillin V (500 mg twice daily for 10 days) or amoxicillin (once daily for 10 days) is first-line per IDSA 2012 guidelines. Group A Strep remains universally sensitive to penicillin. For penicillin allergy, cefalexin, clindamycin, or azithromycin are alternatives depending on the type of allergy.

faqs.question: How long is strep throat contagious?
faqs.answer: Without antibiotics, strep throat is contagious for 2-3 weeks. After starting antibiotics, infectivity drops sharply and most patients are no longer contagious after 24 hours of treatment. Children should stay home from school until they have taken antibiotics for at least 24 hours and are fever-free.

faqs.question: Can adults get strep throat?
faqs.answer: Yes, although strep throat is less common in adults than children. Group A Strep accounts for 5-15% of sore throats in adults. The presentation is the same: sudden onset, fever, tonsillar exudate, and tender glands. Adults with strep are treated with the same antibiotic regimen as children.

faqs.question: What is the fastest way to get rid of a sore throat?
faqs.answer: For viral sore throat: ibuprofen (400 mg every 6-8 hours), warm fluids, salt water gargles, and throat lozenges provide the fastest relief. For bacterial strep throat, starting penicillin or amoxicillin brings fever and pain relief within 24 hours. A single dose of dexamethasone reduces severe throat pain by an average of 7 hours in RCTs.

faqs.question: What is the Centor score for sore throat?
faqs.answer: The Centor/McIsaac score estimates the probability of strep throat. One point each is given for tonsillar exudate, tender anterior neck lymph nodes, fever history, and absence of cough; one point is subtracted for age over 45. Score 0-1: strep very unlikely; 2-3: do a rapid strep test; 4+: consider empirical antibiotics. It is a clinical decision tool, not a test.

faqs.question: Can a sore throat be a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Most sore throats are mild and self-limiting. Seek urgent care for a sore throat with drooling, muffled voice, difficulty opening your mouth, neck swelling, stridor, or swallowing difficulty — these suggest peritonsillar abscess, deep neck infection, or epiglottitis. A sore throat with extreme fatigue and swollen neck glands in a teenager suggests glandular fever (EBV).

faqs.question: Why should I avoid amoxicillin for sore throat in glandular fever?
faqs.answer: Amoxicillin and ampicillin trigger a widespread maculopapular rash in 70-90% of patients with EBV (glandular fever) mononucleosis. This is not a penicillin allergy but a drug-virus interaction. If EBV is suspected, use paracetamol and ibuprofen for symptom relief and avoid all aminopenicillins.

faqs.question: What is a peritonsillar abscess?
faqs.answer: A peritonsillar abscess is a collection of pus between the tonsil and the pharyngeal wall. It causes severe one-sided throat pain, trismus (difficulty opening the mouth), drooling, a hot-potato muffled voice, and uvular deviation away from the affected side. It requires needle aspiration or incision and drainage by an ENT, plus antibiotics.

faqs.question: Does glandular fever (EBV) need antibiotic treatment?
faqs.answer: No. Glandular fever is caused by Epstein-Barr virus and does not respond to antibiotics. Treatment is supportive: rest, analgesics, adequate hydration, and avoidance of alcohol. Steroids are sometimes used for severe tonsillar swelling causing airway compromise. Full recovery takes 2-6 weeks; avoid contact sports for at least 3 weeks due to spleen enlargement.

faqs.question: How many sore throats before a tonsillectomy is recommended?
faqs.answer: The AAO-HNS Paradise criteria recommend considering tonsillectomy for 7 or more episodes per year in one year, 5 or more per year for two consecutive years, or 3 or more per year for three consecutive years. Episodes must be documented by a clinician and include fever, exudate, positive strep test, or cervical lymphadenopathy.

faqs.question: Can pharyngitis cause rheumatic fever?
faqs.answer: Only untreated or inadequately treated Group A Strep pharyngitis causes acute rheumatic fever, not viral sore throats. Rheumatic fever follows 0.3-3% of untreated GABHS cases and can cause permanent heart valve damage. Completing a full 10-day antibiotic course eliminates this risk in over 99% of cases.

faqs.question: What is gonococcal pharyngitis?
faqs.answer: Gonococcal pharyngitis is caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae from oropharyngeal sexual contact. It is often asymptomatic or causes mild sore throat and exudate, indistinguishable from strep. A standard rapid strep test does not detect it. Diagnosis requires a specific throat NAAT or culture. Treatment is ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscular single dose per CDC 2021 STI guidelines.

faqs.question: Is it safe to exercise with a sore throat?
faqs.answer: Light activity is acceptable if you have no fever and feel well enough. Rest is recommended during the febrile phase of bacterial pharyngitis. For infectious mononucleosis, avoid contact sports and strenuous exercise for at least 3 weeks after symptom onset to prevent splenic rupture from an enlarged spleen.
